title: STANDARD FOR CERTAIN PULSES CXS 171-1989 Adopted in 1989. Amended in 2012, 2019.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR CERTAIN PULSES CXS 171-1989 Adopted in 1989. Amended in 2012, 2019.
sections.summary: Document title and adoption/amendment history: Standard for Certain Pulses CXS 171-1989, adopted 1989, amended 2012 and 2019; no substantive provisions in this heading.

Applies to whole, shelled or split pulses intended for direct human consumption and excludes pulses for factory grading/packaging, industrial processing, animal feed, fragmented pulses sold as such, and other legumes covered by separate standards.
Describes pulses as dry seeds of leguminous plants distinguished by low fat content and introduces the product definition and list of pulse species covered by the standard.
Defines pulses as dry seeds of leguminous plants (low-fat) and lists the covered species with scientific names and noted exceptions (beans, lentils, peas, chick peas, field beans, cow peas).

Sets essential composition and quality factors requiring pulses to be safe and suitable for human consumption, free from abnormal flavour/odours and living insects, free from hazardous filth, and subject to specific limits on moisture, extraneous matter and toxic/noxious seeds.

General quality requirements: pulses must be safe and suitable for human consumption, free from abnormal flavours/odours and living insects, and free from filth (animal-origin impurities) in amounts that may pose a health hazard.

sections.section: 3.1.1 Pulses shall be safe and suitable for human consumption.
sections.summary: Obligation that pulses shall be safe and suitable for human consumption.

sections.section: 3.1.2 Pulses shall be free from abnormal flavour, odours, and living insects.
sections.summary: Requires pulses to be free from abnormal flavour, odours, and living insects.

sections.section: 3.1.3 Pulses  shall  be  free  from  filth  (impurities  of  animal  origin,  including  dead  insects)  in  amounts  which  may represent a hazard to human health.
sections.summary: Requires pulses to be free from filth (impurities of animal origin, including dead insects) in amounts that may represent a hazard to human health.

Specific quality factors: provides maximum moisture levels (two sets to suit different climates/storage), specifies extraneous matter and toxic seed requirements, and related handling/storage considerations.

Sets two maximum moisture content levels per pulse type (one for tropical/long-term storage, one for moderate/short-term storage) with a note that lower limits may be required for certain destinations, and provides the percent values by pulse.

sections.section: 3.2.2  Extraneous matter is mineral or organic matter (dust, twigs, seedcoats, seeds of other species, dead insects, fragments, or remains of insects, other impurities of animal origin). Pulses shall have not more than 1 percent extraneous matter of which not more than 0.25 percent shall be mineral matter and not more than 0.10 percent shall be dead insects, fragments or remains of insects, and/or other impurities of animal origin.
sections.summary: Defines extraneous matter (mineral/organic impurities) and sets limits — total extraneous matter ≤1%, mineral matter ≤0.25%, dead insects/animal-origin impurities ≤0.10% — and notes that pulses sold without seed coat shall have maximum moisture content 2 percentage points lower.

Prohibits the presence of specified toxic or noxious seeds (e.g., Crotalaria spp., corn cockle Agrostemma githago, castor bean Ricinus communis, Datura spp.) in amounts that may represent a hazard to human health.
Overview of contaminants requirements for pulses, specifying expectations on heavy metals, pesticide residues and mycotoxins as detailed in subsequent subsections.
Pulses shall be free from heavy metals in amounts that may represent a hazard to health.
Pulses must comply with the maximum pesticide residue limits established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission for this commodity.
Pulses must comply with the maximum mycotoxin limits established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission for this commodity.

Hygiene provisions recommend applying the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene and require, to the extent of good manufacturing practice, that pulses be free from objectionable matter and, when tested, free from microorganisms, parasites, and microorganism-derived substances that may present a health hazard.

sections.section: 5.1 It is recommended that the products covered by the provisions of this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General principles of food hygiene (CXC 1-1969), 1  and other codes of practice recommended by the CAC which are relevant to these products.
sections.summary: Recommendation that pulses be prepared and handled in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and other relevant CAC codes of practice.

sections.section: 5.2 To the extent possible in good manufacturing practice, the products shall be free from objectionable matter.
sections.summary: To the extent possible under good manufacturing practice, pulses shall be free from objectionable matter.

sections.section: 5.3 When tested by appropriate methods of sampling and examination, the products:
sections.summary: When tested by appropriate sampling and examination, pulses shall be free from microorganisms and parasites that may represent a health hazard and shall not contain substances originating from microorganisms in hazardous amounts.

sections.section: 5.3.1 shall be free from microorganisms in amounts which may represent a hazard to health;
sections.summary: Pulses shall be free from microorganisms in amounts that may represent a hazard to health when assessed by appropriate methods of sampling and examination.

sections.section: 5.3.2 shall be free from parasites which may represent a hazard to health; and
sections.summary: Pulses shall be free from parasites that may represent a hazard to health when assessed by appropriate methods of sampling and examination.

sections.section: 5.3.3 shall not contain any substance originating from microorganisms in amounts which may represent a hazard to health.
sections.summary: Pulses shall not contain any substance originating from microorganisms in amounts that may represent a hazard to health when assessed by appropriate methods of sampling and examination.

Packaging requirements for pulses: containers must safeguard hygienic, nutritional, technological and organoleptic qualities, be made of safe suitable materials that do not impart toxins or undesirable odors/flavors, and sacks must be clean, sturdy and securely closed.

sections.section: 6.1 Pulses  shall  be  packaged  in  containers  which  will  safeguard  the  hygienic,  nutritional,  technological,  and organoleptic qualities of the product.
sections.summary: Pulses shall be packaged in containers that safeguard the hygienic, nutritional, technological and organoleptic qualities of the product.

sections.section: 6.2 The containers, including packaging material, shall be made of substances which are safe and suitable for their intended use. They should not impart any toxic substance or undesirable odour or flavour to the product.
sections.summary: Containers and packaging materials for pulses must be made of substances safe and suitable for intended use and must not impart toxic substances or undesirable odour or flavour.

sections.section: 6.3 When the product is packaged in sacks, these must be clean, sturdy and strongly sewn or sealed.
sections.summary: When packaged in sacks, pulses must be in sacks that are clean, sturdy and strongly sewn or sealed.

In addition to the General Standard for labelling (CXS 1-1985), specific labelling provisions require the product name to reflect the commercial type and set rules for information on non-retail containers.
The product name shown on the label shall be the commercial type of the pulse.

For non-retail containers, information may be on the container or in accompanying documents, but the product name, lot identification and manufacturer/packer name and address must appear on the container (which may instead use an identification mark clearly identifiable with accompanying documents).
Compliance with this standard must be checked using the methods of analysis and sampling contained in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to the provisions.
Non-substantive bibliographic notes listing the referenced Codex documents: CXC 1-1969, CXS 1-1985 and CXS 234-1999.

Annex recommends specifying the appropriate factor limit and method when multiple are given and provides visual-examination quality/presentation criteria and limits for pulses, including maximum percentages for seeds with serious defects (max 1%), seeds with slight defects (max 7% with broken pulses ≤3%), broken-pulse definitions, detailed seed discolouration limits (various maxima from 3% to 20% depending on type), and presentation definitions for shelled and split pulses.
